reasoning: The base rate of the World Health Organization characterizing an outbreak as a pandemic is low, historically averaging roughly 1-5% per year (with recent precedents in 2009 for H1N1 and 2020 for COVID-19). While there are elevated concerns regarding the mammalian spread of H5N1 avian influenza, human-to-human transmission remains lacking or extremely limited at this time. Current PHEICs like Mpox are unlikely to be classified by the WHO as a 'pandemic,' a descriptive term generally reserved for explosive, global outbreaks, often of respiratory viruses. Absent current signals of a rapidly globalizing novel pathogen, a probability around 3.5% appropriately accounts for the low annual baseline risk slightly elevated by H5N1 concerns, perfectly aligning with the market freeze value.
